entries.name: Hick's Law
entries.principle: Decision time grows logarithmically with the number of choices presented.
application: Limit primary actions to one per surface.
Chunk secondary options behind progressive disclosure.
Provide sensible defaults to reduce perceived choice.
anti_patterns: 20+ item top-level navigation
10-option settings dropdown without grouping

entries.name: Fitts's Law
entries.principle: Time to acquire a target is a function of distance to and size of the target.
application: Make primary actions large (44px+ touch targets).
Place frequent actions near pointer rest positions or screen edges.
Group related actions so the next click is close to the previous one.
anti_patterns: tiny close buttons in modals
destructive actions next to the default action

entries.name: Doherty Threshold
entries.principle: Productivity soars when computer and user interact at a pace (<400ms) that ensures neither has to wait.
application: Aim for sub-400ms response on every user-initiated action.
Use optimistic UI to acknowledge intent before server confirms.
Show progress for any operation longer than 400ms.
anti_patterns: spinners for sub-second waits
blocking UI on every form submit

entries.name: Tesler's Law (Law of Conservation of Complexity)
entries.principle: Every system has an irreducible amount of complexity; the question is who bears it.
application: Move complexity into product internals when users would not benefit from exposure.
Surface complexity only when the user has a reason to engage with it.
Validate that hiding complexity does not silently change user-meaningful behavior.
anti_patterns: forcing users to configure defaults the product could infer
exposing implementation details in user-facing copy

entries.name: Goal-Gradient Effect
entries.principle: Motivation to reach a goal increases as proximity to the goal increases.
application: Show progress prominently in multi-step flows.
Front-load early easy wins so users feel close to completion.
Surface a percent-complete indicator for long tasks.
anti_patterns: hiding progress in multi-step forms
ambiguous 'almost done' messaging without numbers

entries.name: Choice Overload (Paradox of Choice)
entries.principle: Too many options reduce satisfaction and increase decision paralysis.
application: Recommend a default or top 3 picks before showing the full set.
Use category filters to slice large catalogs.
Allow comparison of at most 3-4 items side by side.
anti_patterns: 100-item pricing-plan comparison
ungrouped settings page with 50+ toggles

entries.name: Postel's Law (Robustness Principle)
entries.principle: Be liberal in what you accept, conservative in what you send.
application: Accept input in multiple formats and normalize internally.
Trim whitespace, normalize phone formats, and accept varying date formats.
Never reject input for formatting if intent is clear.
anti_patterns: rejecting phone numbers without country code instead of inferring
rigid date inputs that reject MM/DD vs DD/MM

entries.name: Sunk Cost Effect
entries.principle: People continue investing in something to justify prior investment, even when abandoning would be better.
application: Acknowledge user progress before asking for restart.
Allow saving partial state so users do not feel they wasted time.
Show recoverable drafts after closing flows mid-way.
anti_patterns: losing form data on session timeout without warning
no autosave during long content creation

entries.name: Miller's Law (7 plus or minus 2)
entries.principle: Working memory holds about 7 (+/- 2) chunks of information at once.
application: Chunk navigation into 5-9 top-level items.
Break long forms into sections of no more than 7 fields.
Group related options under a single label.
anti_patterns: flat 20-item primary nav
30-field forms without sectioning

entries.name: Recognition Over Recall
entries.principle: Minimize memory load by showing options rather than requiring recall.
application: Show recently used or relevant items inline.
Use autocomplete for predictable inputs.
Surface available commands in menus rather than hidden shortcuts.
anti_patterns: command-line-only interfaces without help
search boxes without recent-search suggestions

entries.name: Working Memory Limits
entries.principle: Working memory has limited capacity (~4 items active at once per Cowan's revision).
application: Keep step-to-step dependencies within 3-4 visible items.
Repeat context at decision points rather than relying on prior screens.
Show summary of inputs before final submission.
anti_patterns: multi-page wizards that hide prior answers
confirmation screens with no recap of what was decided

entries.name: Chunking
entries.principle: Grouping related items into meaningful units increases recall and reduces cognitive load.
application: Format phone numbers and account IDs into hyphen-separated groups.
Group form fields by topic with visible separators.
Cluster related menu items under labeled categories.
anti_patterns: 16-digit credit card field with no spacing
unsegmented IBAN inputs

entries.name: Spacing Effect
entries.principle: Learning is more effective when distributed over time rather than crammed.
application: Space onboarding tips across multiple sessions instead of one tour.
Surface tips contextually at the moment of need.
Reinforce key features through repeated, distributed exposures.
anti_patterns: 10-step product tour on first login
single dense help page nobody returns to

entries.name: Attention Tunneling
entries.principle: Users focused on a goal may miss salient information outside their attentional focus.
application: Place critical information directly in the user's task path.
Use motion or contrast sparingly to redirect attention only when needed.
Surface errors near the field that caused them.
anti_patterns: error toasts at top of page while user is at bottom
important warnings in collapsed accordions

entries.name: Banner Blindness
entries.principle: Users ignore elements that resemble advertisements, regardless of relevance.
application: Avoid styling important messages like banner ads.
Place critical information inline with content, not in floating sidebars.
Use restraint with colored callout blocks; reserve them for genuinely critical signals.
anti_patterns: important alerts in colorful right-rail banners
billboard-styled cookie consent on critical pages

entries.name: Inattentional Blindness
entries.principle: Users focused on one task may completely miss unexpected elements.
application: Do not introduce critical UI in peripheral vision during a focused task.
Pause or block the flow when an unmissable decision is required.
Use modal dialogs sparingly but commit fully when used.
anti_patterns: floating side-toaster for irreversible warnings
non-blocking confirmations for destructive actions

entries.name: Gestalt Principle of Proximity
entries.principle: Elements close to each other are perceived as grouped.
application: Use whitespace to separate sections more than borders.
Place labels closer to their inputs than to neighboring fields.
Cluster related cards into bands with consistent inner spacing.
anti_patterns: equal spacing between unrelated elements
labels equidistant from two adjacent fields

entries.name: Gestalt Principle of Similarity
entries.principle: Elements that share visual characteristics are perceived as related.
application: Use a consistent style for elements of the same function.
Reserve a single color and shape language for primary actions.
Break similarity intentionally when distinguishing dangerous actions.
anti_patterns: inconsistent button styles across the same page
same-color destructive and primary buttons

entries.name: Gestalt Principle of Closure
entries.principle: The mind completes partial shapes into recognizable forms.
application: Use suggested edges and partial outlines instead of heavy borders.
Lean on whitespace to define group boundaries without explicit lines.
Trim repeated visual chrome where the brain will fill it in.
anti_patterns: thick borders around every card
boxes-within-boxes nested visual chrome

entries.name: Gestalt Principle of Continuity
entries.principle: Elements arranged on a line or curve are perceived as related.
application: Align elements along consistent axes for scannability.
Use baseline grids to reinforce reading direction.
Connect related steps with a continuous visual line in steppers.
anti_patterns: randomly offset card grids
stepper indicators that do not align

entries.name: Gestalt Principle of Common Region
entries.principle: Elements enclosed within the same boundary are perceived as a group.
application: Use background panels to group related controls.
Apply rounded corners and subtle shading to denote a region without heavy borders.
Separate distinct regions with adequate negative space between them.
anti_patterns: controls floating without container suggesting their grouping
regions defined by color alone without spacing

entries.name: Gestalt Principle of Figure-Ground
entries.principle: Foreground figures stand out from a background; users perceive them as distinct.
application: Maintain consistent figure-ground contrast for interactive elements.
Use elevation (shadows) to separate foreground modal from background canvas.
Mute background content when modals appear.
anti_patterns: modals that blend into background
buttons indistinguishable from surrounding surface

entries.name: Gestalt Principle of Common Fate
entries.principle: Elements that move together are perceived as related.
application: Stagger entry animations of related items in quick succession.
Use synchronized motion to suggest grouping during transitions.
Avoid independent motion of items that should appear unified.
anti_patterns: items in the same group animating at random delays
cards in one list each easing at different durations

entries.name: Law of Pragnanz (Simplicity)
entries.principle: The mind perceives ambiguous images in their simplest possible form.
application: Choose the simplest layout that conveys hierarchy.
Reduce visual noise so the structural shape of the page is the message.
Trim decorative elements that do not aid understanding.
anti_patterns: decorative gradients on every surface
excessive shadows and borders competing for attention

entries.name: Aesthetic-Usability Effect
entries.principle: Users perceive aesthetically pleasing designs as more usable, even when underlying usability is equal.
application: Invest in visual polish for first-impression surfaces.
Maintain typographic consistency to signal care.
Do not let aesthetic mask actual usability problems in research.
anti_patterns: polished surface hiding broken interactions
skipping usability testing because it 'looks good'

entries.name: Cognitive Load Theory
entries.principle: Working memory is constrained; reduce extraneous load to free capacity for the task.
application: Remove decoration that does not aid the task.
Use familiar patterns to lean on existing schemas.
Reveal information progressively rather than all at once.
anti_patterns: dense dashboards showing every metric at once
novel interaction patterns for routine tasks

entries.name: Cognitive Friction
entries.principle: Resistance encountered by the mind when interacting with a product reduces task completion.
application: Match interaction labels to user vocabulary.
Eliminate decisions that have no real consequence.
Reduce the number of distinct concepts a user must learn.
anti_patterns: custom terminology where standard terms exist
asking the user to choose between meaningless variants

entries.name: Confirmation Bias
entries.principle: People prefer information that confirms their existing beliefs.
application: Present balanced data that includes counter-evidence in research summaries.
Default to neutral framing in surveys and analytics dashboards.
Highlight disconfirming signals when they exist.
anti_patterns: dashboards that show only positive metrics
survey questions leading toward a desired answer

entries.name: Anchoring Bias
entries.principle: Initial information disproportionately influences subsequent judgments.
application: Place the recommended price tier visually first.
Show the regular price before discount to anchor savings perception.
Surface a strong default that frames the choice set.
anti_patterns: pricing tables in random order
discount displays without showing the original anchor

entries.name: Default Effect
entries.principle: People tend to accept default options rather than make explicit choices.
application: Set defaults to the most beneficial common choice.
Make defaults reversible to preserve agency.
Document and audit default settings carefully because they shape behavior.
anti_patterns: opt-in defaults for items that benefit only the company
buried defaults that users cannot find later

entries.name: Mental Model Alignment
entries.principle: Users build internal models of how systems work; alignment between user model and system model improves usability.
application: Match information architecture to how users group concepts.
Use research to identify mental models before designing.
When system model must differ, explicitly teach the difference.
anti_patterns: IA based on company org chart instead of user tasks
metaphors that break under closer inspection

entries.name: Jakob's Law
entries.principle: Users spend most of their time on other sites and expect yours to work the same way.
application: Follow established conventions for common patterns (cart, login, search).
Innovate on differentiation, not on conventions users have internalized.
Test deviations from convention with care.
anti_patterns: reinventing the shopping cart icon as something obscure
custom login flows where standard would do

entries.name: Von Restorff Effect (Isolation Effect)
entries.principle: An item that visually differs from its surroundings is more likely to be remembered.
application: Style the recommended pricing tier distinctly from others.
Use a single accent color to highlight the primary action.
Reserve emphasis sparingly so it retains power.
anti_patterns: every button styled to stand out
multiple competing 'recommended' badges

entries.name: Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)
entries.principle: Roughly 80% of effects come from 20% of causes.
application: Identify the 20% of features producing 80% of usage and prioritize their polish.
Audit usage data to find the long tail and consider deprecation.
Optimize the critical path before edge cases.
anti_patterns: polishing rarely used features while top tasks remain rough
equal investment across all features regardless of usage

entries.name: Serial Position Effect
entries.principle: Items at the beginning and end of a list are most easily remembered.
application: Place the most important item first in lists.
Place secondary key items last to ride the recency effect.
Avoid burying critical items in the middle of long lists.
anti_patterns: alphabetical sorting of high-stakes options
critical CTA in the middle of a long form

entries.name: Primacy Effect
entries.principle: Items presented first are more likely to be remembered than items in the middle.
application: Lead with the most important benefit on landing pages.
Open onboarding with the value, not the friction.
Structure error summaries so the most actionable item is first.
anti_patterns: burying value props after company history
error lists in alphabetical order

entries.name: Recency Effect
entries.principle: Items presented last are most easily recalled from short-term memory.
application: End onboarding with the most memorable success moment.
Close pricing pages with the strongest closing argument.
Place the primary CTA at the end of long-form content.
anti_patterns: landing pages that end on a fine-print legal disclaimer
scrolling pages where the CTA is mid-fold and forgotten

entries.name: Zeigarnik Effect
entries.principle: People remember uncompleted tasks better than completed ones.
application: Show partial completion to motivate continuation.
Use progress indicators to keep incomplete tasks salient.
Surface 'in-progress' items at the top of dashboards.
anti_patterns: hiding incomplete tasks
no completion indicator on partially filled forms

entries.name: Peak-End Rule
entries.principle: People judge an experience largely by its peak (best or worst moment) and its end.
application: Invest disproportionately in peak moments (delight, magic, surprise).
Ensure the end of any flow is satisfying, not abrupt.
Pay attention to the post-error recovery experience.
anti_patterns: ending checkout on a plain 'order received' page
long flows that end with a dead screen

entries.name: Weber-Fechner Law
entries.principle: Perceived intensity of a stimulus is proportional to the logarithm of its actual intensity.
application: Use logarithmic scales for chart sizing (bubble area, audio level).
Recognize that small absolute changes feel small unless near a threshold.
Design size and motion increments that step rather than scale linearly.
anti_patterns: linearly mapping circle area to value
expecting users to notice tiny perceptual changes

entries.name: WCAG AA Contrast
entries.principle: Text must have a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1 against its background (3:1 for large text >=18pt).
application: Audit every text-on-color combination against thresholds.
Use tools like Stark or browser DevTools contrast checkers.
Avoid pure-on-pure (white on yellow, black on red) without verification.
anti_patterns: light gray body text on white
low-contrast hover states for interactive elements

entries.name: WCAG AAA Contrast
entries.principle: For AAA conformance, text must meet a 7:1 contrast ratio (4.5:1 for large text).
application: Adopt AAA for high-stakes contexts: medical, legal, government.
Treat AAA as the goal even when AA is the minimum.
Document where AAA cannot be met and why.
anti_patterns: claiming AAA when only AA is met
ignoring contrast on disabled states (still must be readable)

entries.name: Color Blindness Accommodation
entries.principle: Roughly 8% of men and 0.5% of women have some form of color blindness; color cannot be the only signal.
application: Pair color with shape, icon, or pattern for state indication.
Test designs in deuteranopia and protanopia simulations.
Never rely on red-green alone for error vs success.
anti_patterns: red-green-only diff views
stoplight charts without secondary encoding

entries.name: Semantic Color Consistency
entries.source: Material Design and Apple HIG conventions
entries.principle: Reserve specific colors for specific meanings (red=danger, green=success, blue=info).
application: Define a single danger color and use only it for destructive signals.
Do not use semantic colors decoratively.
Document semantic color assignments in your design system.
anti_patterns: red buttons for primary actions where danger is implied
green text used decoratively

entries.name: Color Temperature
entries.principle: Warm colors feel near and active; cool colors feel distant and calm.
application: Use warm accents for energy and urgency moments.
Use cool tones for focus contexts where calm matters.
Stay consistent within a brand's temperature signature.
anti_patterns: mixing warm and cool primaries without intent
warm colors in calm-focused contexts (e.g. meditation app)

entries.name: 60-30-10 Color Rule
entries.principle: Distribute color in approximate 60% dominant, 30% secondary, 10% accent ratio.
application: Reserve the accent color for the single most important action.
Keep the secondary supporting structure (chrome, headings).
Use dominant for canvas and surfaces.
anti_patterns: equal-area distribution of all brand colors
accents on every interactive element

entries.name: Cultural Color Meaning
entries.principle: Colors carry different meanings across cultures (e.g. white = purity in Western, mourning in some Eastern contexts).
application: Research color associations for target markets.
Avoid relying on a single color symbol for global products.
Test color choices with users from each target region.
anti_patterns: assuming Western color meanings globally
red for negative in Chinese markets where it signals fortune

entries.name: Information Color Independence
entries.principle: Information should not be conveyed by color alone.
application: Add icons or text labels to state indicators.
Use underlines or weight changes for links, not color alone.
Provide text alternatives wherever color carries meaning.
anti_patterns: link styled only via color
form errors indicated only by red borders

entries.name: Optimal Line Length (Measure)
entries.source: Bringhurst (2004) - The Elements of Typographic Style
entries.principle: Comfortable reading line length is 45-75 characters per line (66 is the classical optimum).
application: Constrain prose containers to max-width around 65-75ch.
Use max-width on body text in dashboards and articles.
Test reading flow at different viewport widths.
anti_patterns: full-viewport-width body text on desktop
extremely narrow columns under 35 characters

entries.name: Vertical Rhythm
entries.principle: Consistent vertical spacing between baselines creates visual rhythm and aids scanning.
application: Base spacing on a baseline grid derived from body line-height.
Use spacing tokens that are multiples of the base grid.
Align headings, paragraphs, and images to the same rhythm.
anti_patterns: arbitrary spacing between text and images
mismatched line-heights between paragraphs and headings

entries.name: Modular Type Scale
entries.principle: Type sizes derived from a mathematical ratio create visual harmony.
application: Choose a ratio (1.125, 1.25, 1.333, 1.5, 1.618) and derive sizes from it.
Use 5-7 sizes maximum across the entire system.
Document the scale and forbid off-scale type.
anti_patterns: ad-hoc font sizes per component
10 different heading sizes across a product

entries.name: Minimum Body Size 16px
entries.principle: Body text below 16px is hard to read on mobile and small displays.
application: Set base font-size to at least 16px (1rem).
Use rem-based scaling so users can adjust system size.
Reserve 14px or smaller for metadata, captions, and chrome only.
anti_patterns: 12px body text
fixed pixel sizes that ignore user preferences

entries.name: Type Pair Contrast
entries.principle: Pair contrasting (not similar) type families: a display + a body, not two display fonts.
application: Pair a humanist serif with a clean sans-serif, or vice versa.
Avoid pairing two fonts of the same classification.
Maintain consistent character widths in mono pairs.
anti_patterns: two sans-serifs with subtle differences
using one font for everything when contrast would help

entries.name: Line Height 1.4-1.6 for Body
entries.principle: Body text line-height between 1.4 and 1.6 is the comfortable reading range.
application: Default body line-height to 1.5.
Increase line-height slightly for sans-serif body fonts.
Tighten line-height for headings (1.1-1.3).
anti_patterns: line-height of 1.0 for body text
line-height of 2.0 across paragraphs

entries.name: Tracking Adjusted by Size
entries.principle: Larger sizes need tighter tracking; smaller sizes benefit from looser tracking.
application: Apply negative letter-spacing to display sizes (-0.02em to -0.04em).
Apply slight positive tracking to all-caps small labels (+0.05em).
Tune tracking per typeface; defaults are not always right.
anti_patterns: same letter-spacing across all text sizes
loose tracking on display headlines

entries.name: Hierarchy via Weight, Not Just Size
entries.principle: Type hierarchy emerges from weight, size, color, and spacing together.
application: Use weight contrast (400 vs 700) more than size jumps within content blocks.
Combine weight with color (ink vs muted) for secondary emphasis.
Avoid relying solely on size for emphasis.
anti_patterns: bold-only emphasis with no color or size variation
huge size jumps where weight contrast would suffice

entries.name: 8-Point Grid System
entries.principle: Spacing values derived from multiples of 8px create visual harmony across screens.
application: Define spacing tokens as 4, 8, 16, 24, 32, 48, 64.
Avoid off-grid values unless necessary for optical alignment.
Apply the grid to both component spacing and layout spacing.
anti_patterns: arbitrary 13px or 27px gaps
mixing 8px and 10px-based scales inconsistently

entries.name: Golden Ratio (1.618)
entries.principle: The 1.618 ratio appears in classical proportions and is often perceived as harmonious.
application: Use 1.618 as the ratio between primary and secondary column widths.
Apply to image aspect ratios in editorial layouts.
Use it as a type-scale ratio for visually expressive systems.
anti_patterns: forcing golden ratio in contexts where 8pt grid is cleaner
treating it as mystical rather than one option among many

entries.name: Rule of Thirds
entries.principle: Dividing a composition into thirds creates visually balanced placement points.
application: Place key focal points at third intersections in hero imagery.
Use third-based grids for editorial layouts.
Avoid centering everything; offset to a third for visual interest.
anti_patterns: centered subjects in every hero image
perfect symmetry in editorial design

entries.name: Optical Alignment
entries.principle: Mathematical alignment is not always perceived as aligned; adjust optically for visual correctness.
application: Shift round shapes slightly past edges to appear aligned.
Adjust quotation marks and asymmetric letterforms by eye.
Pull icon glyphs into baselines based on perceived weight.
anti_patterns: mechanically centered text that looks off-center
pixel-perfect alignment ignoring perceptual cues

entries.name: Whitespace Density Management
entries.principle: Generous whitespace reads as quality and clarity; cramped layouts feel busy and untrustworthy.
application: Increase outer padding on premium and editorial surfaces.
Allow components to breathe even on dense dashboards.
Use whitespace as an active design element, not leftover space.
anti_patterns: squeezing every pixel for content density
treating whitespace as wasted real estate

entries.name: Layout Grid Consistency
entries.principle: A consistent underlying grid creates structural coherence across pages.
application: Define a 12-column grid with consistent gutters across breakpoints.
Snap all blocks to the grid; document exceptions explicitly.
Use container queries and grid templates for responsive consistency.
anti_patterns: mixing 12 and 16 column grids on adjacent pages
off-grid floats and absolute positioning everywhere

entries.name: F-Pattern Reading
entries.principle: Users scan content-heavy pages in an F-shape: across the top, then partway down, then briefly down the left.
application: Place the most important info top-left.
Start paragraphs with the key phrase (front-load).
Use bulleted lists and bold first words to support scanning.
anti_patterns: burying key value below the F-fold
long paragraphs without front-loaded key information

entries.name: Z-Pattern Reading
entries.principle: On sparse, visual-first pages, users scan in a Z-shape: top-left to top-right, then diagonally to bottom-left, then bottom-right.
application: Place the logo top-left, primary nav top-right.
Place the CTA at the bottom-right of hero sections.
Use Z-pattern for landing pages, not for dense content.
anti_patterns: primary CTA hidden top-left where users start
Z-pattern applied to dense article content

entries.name: 300ms Response Threshold
entries.principle: UI responses faster than 300ms feel instant; longer needs feedback.
application: Target 100-300ms for transitions and feedback.
Use spring or ease-out curves to feel responsive.
Show immediate visual change on press, not just on release.
anti_patterns: 500ms+ button feedback animations
no immediate response on tap or press

entries.name: 1-Second Patience Window
entries.principle: Users tolerate up to 1 second before flow breaks; beyond, they need explicit progress.
application: Show a spinner or skeleton if a task exceeds 1 second.
Use estimated progress for tasks exceeding 10 seconds.
Break long tasks into visible sub-steps.
anti_patterns: 10-second blank screens with no feedback
spinners for 200ms operations (jitter)

entries.name: Prefers Reduced Motion
entries.principle: Some users have vestibular disorders or motion sensitivity; honor their system preference.
application: Respect the prefers-reduced-motion media query.
Replace parallax and large-distance transforms with instant fades.
Default reduced-motion behavior should still convey meaning.
anti_patterns: parallax that cannot be disabled
motion required to convey meaning

entries.name: Easing Curve Selection
entries.source: Material Design Motion; Apple HIG Animation
entries.principle: Easing shapes how motion feels: ease-out enters smoothly, ease-in exits sharply, springs feel physical.
application: Use ease-out (or out-expo) for entries; user attention is rewarded immediately.
Use ease-in for exits when items should accelerate offscreen.
Use spring curves for natural, physical-feeling interactions.
anti_patterns: linear timing on all transitions
bounce easing on functional micro-interactions

entries.name: Stagger Delay
entries.source: Disney 12 Principles of Animation; Material Motion
entries.principle: Sequentially delaying animations in a group creates a sense of order and rhythm.
application: Stagger list-item entry by 30-60ms intervals.
Cap stagger total at 600ms so the last item is not annoyingly late.
Use stagger to imply common-fate grouping.
anti_patterns: all items animating simultaneously (loses hierarchy)
stagger delays over 100ms (feels slow)

entries.name: Motion Hierarchy
entries.principle: Motion guides attention and signals importance just as visual hierarchy does.
application: Reserve large or dramatic motion for primary moments.
Use subtle motion for ambient or background changes.
Match motion intensity to the action's significance.
anti_patterns: dramatic motion on every micro-interaction
all motion at the same intensity

entries.name: Doherty Threshold Applied to Animation
entries.source: Doherty and Thadhani (1982); modern motion design
entries.principle: Animations longer than 400ms feel slow and break the productivity flow.
application: Cap UI transitions at 300-400ms maximum.
Use 150-250ms for hover and press feedback.
Reserve 500ms+ only for ceremonial or onboarding moments.
anti_patterns: 800ms modal entries
1s+ page transitions

entries.name: 100ms Perception Threshold
entries.principle: Users perceive responses under 100ms as instantaneous.
application: Target sub-100ms response for press, hover, and focus feedback.
Pre-load and cache to keep navigation feeling instant.
Use local optimistic updates for actions that take longer.
anti_patterns: delayed press-down feedback
hover delays over 100ms

entries.name: Inline Validation
entries.principle: Validating fields inline as users complete them reduces error rates compared to submit-time validation.
application: Validate on blur (after user leaves the field), not on every keystroke.
Show success indicators where helpful (e.g. password strength).
Surface errors directly below the field with a clear fix.
anti_patterns: validation only on submit for a 20-field form
aggressive per-keystroke validation that scolds typing

entries.name: Single-Column Form Layout
entries.principle: Single-column forms are completed faster and more accurately than multi-column.
application: Default to single-column layout for all forms.
Reserve side-by-side only for closely related pairs (first/last name).
Maintain a consistent vertical alignment.
anti_patterns: two-column forms with unrelated fields side by side
forms that snake left-to-right then back

entries.name: Required vs Optional Marking
entries.principle: Mark whichever set (required or optional) is smaller; favor marking optional in modern UX.
application: If most fields are required, mark only the optional ones.
Use the word 'optional' rather than '*' for clarity.
Document required state programmatically (aria-required).
anti_patterns: asterisks on every field in a fully required form
no marking at all

entries.name: Label Above Field
entries.principle: Labels placed above fields are completed faster than labels beside or inside fields.
application: Default to label-above for all forms.
Reserve floating labels for very dense surfaces only.
Never use placeholder text as the only label (accessibility hazard).
anti_patterns: placeholder-only inputs that lose context on focus
labels right-aligned beside fields

entries.name: Specific Error Messages
entries.principle: Errors must say what went wrong and what to do about it, in plain language.
application: Name the field, the problem, and the fix in the error message.
Avoid generic 'form contains errors' summaries; surface per-field.
Pass through server validation messages verbatim where useful.
anti_patterns: 'invalid input' with no detail
'form contains errors' with no field-level guidance

entries.name: Input Affordance
entries.source: Norman (1988) - The Design of Everyday Things
entries.principle: An input should look like an input: bordered, fillable, and visually distinct from non-interactive text.
application: Use visible borders or distinct backgrounds to mark input fields.
Avoid borderless or flat inputs unless context makes them obvious.
Provide hover, focus, and disabled states that reinforce affordance.
anti_patterns: text fields styled like static text
buttons that do not look clickable

entries.name: Smart Defaults
entries.principle: Pre-fill safe, common, or inferable values to reduce user effort.
application: Pre-fill country from IP or browser locale (always overridable).
Default newsletter opt-in based on context; never sneak it.
Pre-select the most common timezone, currency, or language.
anti_patterns: empty country dropdowns starting at A
no defaults on multi-step setup

entries.name: Field Grouping
entries.principle: Group related fields visually to reduce cognitive load and improve scanning.
application: Use fieldset/legend pairs for semantic grouping.
Add visible section dividers between groups.
Limit each section to 5-7 fields where possible.
anti_patterns: one long undifferentiated field list
groupings that lump unrelated fields together

entries.name: 44px Minimum Touch Target
entries.principle: Tappable elements should be at least 44x44px to be reliably hit by a fingertip.
application: Set min-height and min-width 44px on all tap targets.
Add invisible padding on small icons to extend hit area.
Apply universally to mobile and tablet contexts.
anti_patterns: 16px icon buttons in mobile toolbars
links smaller than thumbtip

entries.name: 48dp Minimum Touch Target (Android)
entries.principle: Android touch targets should be at least 48dp x 48dp.
application: Match the Material Design 48dp minimum on Android contexts.
Use dp/sp for scalable sizes that respect user font preferences.
Treat 44-48 as equivalent on cross-platform products.
anti_patterns: mixing iOS and Android target sizes inconsistently
ignoring dp scaling on Android

entries.name: Thumb Zone Reachability
entries.source: Hoober (2013) - How Do Users Really Hold Mobile Devices
entries.principle: Most users one-hand their phones; comfortable reach is the bottom 2/3 of the screen.
application: Place primary actions in the bottom-third reachable zone.
Avoid placing critical taps in the top-left corner.
Consider thumb dominance (right-handed bias in many regions).
anti_patterns: primary CTA top-left on mobile
navigation bars users cannot reach without two hands

entries.name: Gesture vs Button Tradeoff
entries.principle: Gestures are efficient but discoverable only through teaching; buttons are obvious but take more space.
application: Pair gestures with discoverable button equivalents.
Reserve novel gestures for delight, not core actions.
Teach gestures through first-use tooltips or coachmarks.
anti_patterns: hidden gestures for critical paths with no button fallback
swipe-only deletion with no visible affordance

entries.name: Bottom Sheet Pattern
entries.principle: Bottom sheets bring contextual content into the thumb zone while keeping background visible.
application: Use bottom sheets for filters, actions, and short flows.
Allow drag-to-dismiss as the natural mobile gesture.
Reserve full-screen modals for focused multi-step flows.
anti_patterns: top-anchored modals on mobile (out of thumb reach)
bottom sheets with too much content (becomes a page)

entries.name: Pull to Refresh
entries.principle: Pull-to-refresh is a learned mobile convention for refreshing top-of-list content.
application: Implement pull-to-refresh for feed and list views.
Add visible affordance (arrow, spinner) on pull.
Pair with explicit refresh option for accessibility.
anti_patterns: pull-to-refresh on non-list contexts
missing pull-to-refresh on chronological feeds

entries.name: Mobile Viewport Configuration
entries.source: W3C CSS Device Adaptation; Apple Safari guides
entries.principle: Set the viewport meta tag correctly so mobile browsers render at device width.
application: Use width=device-width, initial-scale=1 in the viewport meta.
Do not disable user zoom (maximum-scale=1 hurts accessibility).
Test layouts at 320px (small phones) and 768px (tablets).
anti_patterns: maximum-scale=1 disabling zoom
fixed pixel widths that break under 360px

entries.name: Tap and Hold Delay
entries.source: Apple HIG; Material Design touch guidelines
entries.principle: Tap-and-hold gestures require a recognized delay (around 500ms) to trigger contextual actions.
application: Reserve long-press for power-user shortcuts, not critical paths.
Provide visual feedback during the hold (pulse, halo).
Pair with an alternative discoverable affordance.
anti_patterns: only way to delete is long-press with no other option
long-press triggers without visual feedback

entries.name: Keyboard Navigation
entries.principle: All interactive elements must be reachable and operable via keyboard.
application: Verify tab order matches visual order.
Implement Tab, Shift+Tab, Enter, Space, Arrow keys per ARIA APG.
Test by unplugging the mouse.
anti_patterns: dropdowns that only open on hover
modals where Tab cycles outside

entries.name: Focus Management
entries.principle: Visible focus indicators and intentional focus transitions are required for keyboard users.
application: Never remove focus outlines without replacing with a visible alternative.
Move focus into modals on open; return to trigger on close.
Trap focus inside modal dialogs.
anti_patterns: outline:none with no replacement
modals that leak focus to background

entries.name: ARIA Labels and Roles
entries.principle: ARIA attributes provide assistive technology with information not conveyed by native HTML.
application: Prefer native HTML over ARIA when possible.
Use aria-label and aria-labelledby on controls lacking visible text.
Validate ARIA usage with axe or WAVE.
anti_patterns: role='button' on a real <button>
icon-only buttons with no accessible name

entries.name: Screen Reader Compatibility
entries.source: W3C WCAG; NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver guidelines
entries.principle: Content must convey meaning when read non-visually.
application: Test with NVDA, VoiceOver, or TalkBack on actual flows.
Provide text alternatives for all non-decorative imagery.
Use semantic landmarks (header, nav, main, footer).
anti_patterns: images of text with no alt
interactive divs with no role

entries.name: Skip-to-Content Link
entries.principle: Provide a skip link so keyboard users can bypass repeated navigation.
application: Place skip link as the first focusable element on the page.
Show it visibly on focus (it can be visually hidden otherwise).
Target the main content landmark with a clear id.
anti_patterns: no skip link on pages with extensive header nav
skip link broken (target missing)

entries.name: Semantic HTML
entries.principle: Use the correct HTML element for the content's purpose.
application: Use <button> for actions, <a> for navigation.
Use heading hierarchy (h1-h6) reflecting document structure.
Use <nav>, <main>, <article>, <aside> landmarks.
anti_patterns: <div onclick> instead of <button>
skipping heading levels (h1 then h3)

entries.name: Alt Text Discipline
entries.principle: Alt text should communicate the content and function of an image, not describe it filename-style.
application: Decorative images get alt=''.
Functional images describe the action (e.g. alt='Search').
Content images describe what matters for the surrounding context.
anti_patterns: alt='image123.jpg'
decorative images with alt='decorative'

entries.name: ARIA Live Regions
entries.principle: Dynamic content updates must be announced to screen readers via live regions.
application: Use aria-live='polite' for non-urgent updates (form-save).
Use aria-live='assertive' for urgent updates (errors).
Use role='status' or role='alert' as appropriate.
anti_patterns: error toasts invisible to screen readers
form updates that vanish before being announced

entries.name: Halo Effect
entries.principle: Positive impressions in one area influence perception in unrelated areas.
application: Invest in first-impression surfaces (landing, sign-up).
Maintain consistent quality across product surfaces.
Acknowledge that a polished docs site raises trust in the entire product.
anti_patterns: polished marketing site, neglected in-app polish
ignoring small details on critical paths

entries.name: Social Proof
entries.principle: People look to others' behavior to guide their own decisions.
application: Show customer logos and testimonials on conversion pages.
Use 'X people are looking at this' patterns carefully and honestly.
Highlight number of users, reviews, or downloads where relevant.
anti_patterns: fake or exaggerated proof
logo soup with no context

entries.name: Authority Bias
entries.principle: People give more weight to opinions and signals from authority figures.
application: Display industry certifications, security badges, expert endorsements.
Use authoritative voice in critical content (medical, legal, financial).
Reference research and sources where claims are made.
anti_patterns: overstating credentials
fake trust badges with no actual certification

entries.name: Reciprocity Principle
entries.principle: People feel obligated to return what they receive; gifts and value upfront build relationships.
application: Offer genuine free value before asking for commitment.
Provide useful free tools, content, or community.
Avoid manipulative 'free trial' tactics that feel like traps.
anti_patterns: paywalled key info that should be free
fake free tools that gate the result

entries.name: Familiarity Effect (Mere Exposure)
entries.principle: Repeated exposure to something increases preference for it.
application: Maintain consistent visual identity across surfaces.
Reuse familiar patterns within the product.
Build distinctive brand assets that reward repeated viewing.
anti_patterns: redesigning core surfaces too frequently
inconsistent branding across product touchpoints

entries.name: Loss Aversion
entries.principle: Losses feel roughly twice as bad as equivalent gains feel good.
application: Frame value as preventing a loss when relevant ('do not lose your data').
Use loss aversion ethically; do not manufacture artificial scarcity.
Surface what users keep when they upgrade vs what they lose if they downgrade.
anti_patterns: fake countdowns and artificial urgency
fear-driven copy that does not reflect real stakes

entries.name: Trust Signals
entries.principle: Visible markers of trust (security badges, payment logos, return policies) reduce friction at decision points.
application: Place trust signals near commitment moments (checkout, sign-up).
Show SSL, PCI, GDPR, SOC2 indicators where genuine.
Make refund and return policies findable, not buried.
anti_patterns: trust badges in footer only, not at checkout
hiding return policy behind multiple clicks

entries.name: Cognitive Ease (System 1 vs System 2)
entries.principle: Easy-to-process content feels more credible than effortful content.
application: Use simple, familiar language for trust-critical claims.
Maintain high readability (legible type, sufficient contrast).
Reduce cognitive effort for users making evaluation judgments.
anti_patterns: dense legal copy on the homepage
complex pricing tables with hard-to-compare units

entries.name: Progressive Disclosure
entries.principle: Show only what is needed at each moment; reveal advanced options on demand.
application: Start with the simplest viable version of the interface.
Use 'Show advanced' toggles for power-user options.
Tier complexity so casual users never see expert noise.
anti_patterns: showing every setting on first load
burying primary actions among expert options

entries.name: Empty States
entries.principle: Empty states should orient, motivate, and guide the user to a first action.
application: Show why the section is empty and what action populates it.
Provide a primary CTA to fill the empty state.
Use empty state as a teaching moment for the feature.
anti_patterns: literally blank screens with no guidance
empty states that say 'No data' and nothing else

entries.name: Skeleton vs Spinner Loading
entries.principle: Skeleton screens reduce perceived wait time more than spinners by hinting at incoming content shape.
application: Use skeletons for content-heavy views (lists, articles, dashboards).
Use spinners for short, indeterminate actions (button submits).
Match skeleton shapes to actual content layout.
anti_patterns: spinners on full-page loads
generic skeletons that do not match real content

entries.name: Optimistic UI
entries.source: Linear engineering blog (2019); modern SPA patterns
entries.principle: Update the UI as if the action succeeded; reconcile if the server disagrees.
application: Update local state immediately on user action.
Show a subtle state if the action is still pending.
Reconcile gracefully if the server rejects (show clear error and revert).
anti_patterns: waiting for server before any visual feedback
optimistic update that silently fails

entries.name: Confirmation Dialogs (Used Sparingly)
entries.principle: Confirmation dialogs train users to dismiss them; reserve for irreversible high-cost actions only.
application: Use undo over confirmation where possible.
Reserve confirms for delete, dispatch, payment-finalize.
Word confirmation clearly: name the action and its consequence.
anti_patterns: confirming every save
confirmation with ambiguous 'Are you sure?' wording

entries.name: Undo Over Confirm
entries.principle: Offering an undo after an action is less disruptive than a pre-action confirmation.
application: Implement undo for delete, archive, and most reversible actions.
Keep undo window long enough for human reaction (5-10s).
Make undo discoverable via a toast or status bar.
anti_patterns: confirming every delete instead of allowing undo
undo windows of 1-2s (too short to react)

entries.name: Wizard vs Single Page
entries.principle: Long forms benefit from wizard segmentation; short forms benefit from single-page directness.
application: Use wizards for 10+ field flows or multi-section setups.
Use single page for 3-7 field forms.
Show progress and allow back navigation in wizards.
anti_patterns: 3-field wizards (overkill)
30-field single-page forms

entries.name: Onboarding Patterns
entries.principle: Effective onboarding teaches via doing, not via tours; defers complexity until needed.
application: Get the user to a first success moment as fast as possible.
Replace tours with contextual hints at the moment of need.
Use empty-state CTAs as in-product onboarding.
anti_patterns: 10-step product tours on first login
modal interruptions that block the first task
